    Daily Summaries
    • March 18, 2026

    astro-ph.GA Daily Summary — March 18, 2026

    Eight papers spanning cosmic dawn to the local universe, from dust-reddened galaxies at z = 11 to the kinematics of the Coma Cluster. Today's highlights: a "red monster" confirmed at record redshift, a Type Ia supernova displaced by a collisional ring, and the first spatially resolved magnetic field measurement inside an elliptical galaxy's Bondi sphere.

    🌌 Galaxy Environments & Evolution

    Three papers paint a consistent picture of how environment reshapes galaxies at every scale. In the Coma Cluster, new DESI kinematics reveal the orbital geometry of quenching in real time — blue galaxies fall in on radial orbits and get stripped into the red sequence. At the group scale, the M51 system shows that even low-mass groups can erase the radial gradients we associate with undisturbed disk growth, challenging the assumption that environmental processing requires cluster-scale halos. And in the most dramatic case, a head-on galaxy collision physically displaces old stellar populations into expanding rings — creating Type Ia supernovae that appear hostless at cosmological distances. Together, these results remind us that environment acts through multiple channels simultaneously: stripping gas, flattening gradients, and literally ejecting stars from their birthplaces.

    • Pedratti et al. — DESI kinematics of 1651 Coma galaxies show blue cloud members plunging through the cluster on radial orbits, providing direct kinematic evidence for ram-pressure stripping as the driver of the blue → green → red evolutionary sequence.
    • Thainá-Batista et al. — J-PLUS resolved maps of the M51 group reveal flattened age and metallicity gradients compared to the pristine M101 group, with M63 showing outside-in quenching from a past encounter.
    • O'Connor et al. — A Type Ia supernova in a collisional ring 11.4 kpc from its host shows that galaxy collisions can dynamically displace old progenitors, identifying ring galaxies as "supernova factories" with implications for the hostless SN problem.

    🔴 The High-Redshift Frontier

    The most distant spectroscopically confirmed dust-reddened galaxy pushes the timeline for dust enrichment to just 400 Myr after the Big Bang. For galaxy evolution, this matters because it means current rest-frame UV surveys may be systematically missing a significant fraction of early star formation happening behind dust — with direct consequences for cosmic SFR density estimates, stellar mass functions at z > 10, and reionization models. If the "red monster" phase accounts for ~20% of early galaxy lifetimes as predicted, the true diversity of the first galaxies is far richer than UV-selected samples suggest.

    • Rodighiero et al. — Spectroscopic confirmation of EGS-z11-R0 at z = 11.45 with red UV slope (β ≈ −1.0) and tentative [Fe V] detection, making it the highest-redshift "red monster" — a dust-enshrouded galaxy assembling its stellar mass before radiation clears the dust.

    ⭐ Star Formation & ISM Physics

    Two papers address the initial conditions of star formation — the physics that ultimately sets the stellar mass function and feedback budget in galaxies. The fractal structure inherited by young clusters from their parent clouds gets erased on a universal timescale (~2.5 free-fall times), suggesting a fundamental gravitational clock governs cluster assembly regardless of environment. Meanwhile, new polarimetry of an infrared dark cloud catches a massive filament at the transcritical boundary where magnetic, gravitational, and kinetic energies are all comparable — the tipping point that determines whether and how massive stars form. These results constrain the sub-galactic physics that feeds back into galaxy-scale evolution.

    • Akhmetali et al. — Simulations show young star clusters erase their inherited fractal substructure by ~2.5 free-fall times through dynamical relaxation — a timescale that appears universal across cloud masses.
    • Jadhav et al. — SOFIA/HAWC+ reveals an hourglass magnetic field in IRDC G351.77−0.53 with mass-to-flux ratio λ ≈ 0.9, placing this massive filament right at the transcritical boundary between magnetic support and gravitational collapse.

    🚀 AGN Jets & Non-Thermal Physics

    Two papers reshape our understanding of how AGN jets interact with their surroundings — a key ingredient in the feedback cycle that regulates star formation in massive galaxies. A new theoretical framework shows that recollimation shocks in sub-relativistic jets can accelerate particles to PeV–EeV energies, meaning even modest Seyfert jets could inject significant non-thermal energy into the circumgalactic medium. Complementing this, multi-decade VLBI polarimetry of 3C 84 reveals that the Faraday rotation screen isn't the accretion flow but the ambient circumnuclear medium — giving us the first spatially resolved magnetic field profile inside the Bondi sphere of an elliptical galaxy. Together, these constrain both the energy input from jets and the magnetized environment they propagate through.

    • Peretti et al. — Semi-analytic modeling promotes recollimation shocks to dominant particle accelerators across jet classes, predicting PeV–EeV proton energies in Seyfert jets with hadronic signatures matching recent HAWC and LHAASO detections.
    • Park et al. — Nine years of multi-frequency VLBI show all rotation measures in 3C 84 follow RM ∝ r−2.7 from 1–15 pc, tracing the ambient medium within the Bondi sphere rather than a RIAF — yielding B ∝ r−1.7 to −2.2, far steeper than M87.

    8 papers analyzed. 80+ wiki pages in the Atlas. Browse all papers →
